concept_name: The 's' in Kinematic Equations Is DISPLACEMENT, Not Distance
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 6.5 — Equations of Motion; split from distance_vs_displacement bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A cricketer throws a ball straight up at forty metres per second on an empty ground in Delhi. After six seconds, where is the ball? The kinematic equation s = u t + half a t squared gives a number — but that number is the DISPLACEMENT, meaning 'sixty metres above the start', not the total distance the ball travelled (which would count both the rise and the descent). Using the equation wrongly can lead to completely different answers.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A car on the Mumbai-Pune Expressway brakes sharply. Using v squared equals u squared plus two a s, you solve for s — but the s you get is the displacement from the moment of braking to the stop, not the total distance the car's brakes scraped, which may include a skid that you did not account for.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An elevator dropping at five metres per second decelerates at two metres per second squared over three seconds. The s in v = u + a t plugged into s = u t + half a t squared tells you how far BELOW its starting floor the elevator is — a signed displacement. If you wanted the total distance it moved (say for cable-wear calculations), you would need to track motion in each sub-interval separately.

formulas.eq1: v = u + a t     (change of velocity)
formulas.eq2: s = u t + (1/2) a t^2   (s is DISPLACEMENT)
formulas.eq3: v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s   (s is DISPLACEMENT here too)
formulas.note: Distance requires summing |ds/dt| over time; it equals s only when direction never reverses

constraints: All three kinematic equations take s as signed DISPLACEMENT, NOT distance
If u and a have opposite signs, the motion reverses and distance > |displacement|
To compute distance when motion reverses: split the motion into 'before reversal' and 'after reversal' pieces and sum |s| in each
Equations are valid only for constant acceleration

STATE_1.title: Ball Thrown Up — Plug-And-Chug Gives the Wrong Answer

scene_composition.text:
Setup

  u = +40 m/s  (thrown straight up)
  a = -9.8 m/s^2  (gravity, down)
  t = 6 s

Student asks: total DISTANCE after 6 s?

Plug into s = u t + 0.5 a t^2
  s = 40 * 6 + 0.5 * (-9.8) * 36
    = 240 - 176.4
    = +63.6 m

So distance = 63.6 m?
No — 63.6 is a displacement,
not the distance covered.

What actually happens

At t = 4.08 s the ball reaches
peak height:
  t_peak = u / |a| = 40 / 9.8 ~ 4.08 s
  h_peak = u^2 / (2|a|) ~ 81.6 m

Then it starts coming down.
At t = 6 s it is at +63.6 m
(still 63.6 m above the throw point).

Distance traveled in 6 s
  up (81.6 m) + back down (81.6 - 63.6 = 18 m)
  = 99.6 m

So distance (99.6) > displacement (63.6).
Ball Thrown Up — Plug-And-Chug Gives the Wrong Answer

tts_sentences.text_en: A cricketer throws a ball straight up at forty metres per second. Six seconds later, a student asks: how much distance has the ball covered?

First instinct — plug into s equals u t plus half a t squared. Forty times six is two hundred forty. Half times minus nine point eight times thirty-six is minus one seventy-six point four. Sum is sixty-three point six metres.
Claim: distance is sixty-three point six metres. That answer is WRONG.

The ball rises to about eighty-one point six metres at peak, then starts coming down. At six seconds, it is sixty-three point six metres above the throw point — that is the DISPLACEMENT, not the distance.
The distance is eighty-one point six up plus eighteen back down, equals ninety-nine point six metres. Nowhere near sixty-three point six.
The kinematic equation gave the right displacement. It did NOT give the distance. This is the most common student mistake in kinematics problems.

STATE_2.title: Why the Equation Gives Displacement — The Reversal Gets Cancelled

scene_composition.text:
s = u t + (1/2) a t^2

Derivation of this formula
integrates VELOCITY over time:

  s = integral of v(t) dt

When v is positive, ds is positive.
When v is negative, ds is negative.

Up motion contributes +81.6.
Down motion contributes -18.
Sum = +63.6 = displacement.

The negative contribution CANCELS
part of the positive one, leaving
only the signed shift — displacement.

Distance needs |v|, not v

  distance = integral of |v(t)| dt

The absolute value converts
downward motion back to positive
contribution.

For the ball example
  up leg   +81.6
  down leg +18
  sum      99.6 m

There is no single kinematic
equation for distance when direction
reverses. You MUST split the motion
into sub-intervals.
Why the Equation Gives Displacement — The Reversal Gets Cancelled

tts_sentences.text_en: Understand why the equation behaves this way. The formula s equals u t plus half a t squared comes from integrating velocity over time.
When velocity is positive, small bits of motion add positively. When velocity is negative, they add negatively. The sum is the NET shift — the displacement.

For the ball, upward motion contributes plus eighty-one point six metres. Downward motion contributes minus eighteen metres. Sum is plus sixty-three point six. The negative piece cancelled part of the positive piece.
Distance needs the absolute value of velocity. Every piece, whether up or down, adds positively. That gives ninety-nine point six metres.

There is no single closed-form kinematic equation for distance when the motion reverses. You have to split the motion into sub-intervals — one per monotonic piece — and sum the absolute displacements.

STATE_3.title: The Workflow — Always Interpret s as Displacement

scene_composition.text:
Workflow for kinematics problems

(1) Pick a sign convention
    (e.g. up = +).

(2) Write out u, a, t with signs.

(3) Apply s = u t + 0.5 a t^2 directly.
    The s you compute IS the
    SIGNED displacement. That is what
    the formula guarantees.

(4) If the question asks for DISTANCE:
    (4a) Find t when v = 0 (reversal).
    (4b) Compute |displacement| over
         each no-reversal interval.
    (4c) Sum those absolute values.
    (4d) That sum is the distance.
The Workflow — Always Interpret s as Displacement
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the safe workflow. Always interpret s as displacement, then convert to distance only when the question asks.
Step one — pick a sign convention. Step two — write u, a, and t with signs. Step three — apply the kinematic equation. What it gives you is the signed displacement.
If the question asks for displacement, you are done. If it asks for distance, step four — find any time when velocity is zero, because that is a turning point.
Step four-b — compute the displacement magnitude over each no-reversal interval. Step four-c — sum those magnitudes.

That sum is the distance. Never short-cut this by plugging into the kinematic equation directly — the direct plug gives displacement, not distance. Remembering this single rule prevents the most common sign-related mistakes on JEE problems.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The s in s = ut + 0.5 a t^2 gives total distance covered
trigger_phrases: ut plus half at squared distance
why does my distance formula give wrong answer

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Kinematic 's' Is Total Distance

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

In class we saw s = ut + 0.5 a t^2.
Students generally read 's' as 'distance'.

Ball thrown at 40 m/s, t = 6 s
  s = 40(6) + 0.5(-9.8)(36)
    = 63.6

So distance = 63.6 m?

Actually, the ball actually went up 81.6 m,
came down to 63.6 m -> distance is 99.6 m.

The formula gave displacement, not distance.
Your Belief — Kinematic 's' Is Total Distance
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A very common reading of the kinematic equation — 's' is distance, the total amount the object moved.
For the ball thrown at forty metres per second, the equation gives sixty-three point six. So the student says distance is sixty-three point six.

The real distance is ninety-nine point six, because the ball went up eighty-one point six and then came back down eighteen metres. The equation gave displacement — the net shift — not the accumulated path.

STATE_2.title: The Split Method — Distance After Reversal

scene_composition.text:
Finding the actual distance

Step 1  When does v reach zero?
  0 = u + a t_peak
  t_peak = -u / a = 40 / 9.8 ~ 4.08 s

Step 2  Displacement from 0 to t_peak
  s1 = 40(4.08) + 0.5(-9.8)(4.08)^2
     = 163.2 - 81.6
     = 81.6 m   (ascent)

Step 3  Displacement from t_peak to 6 s
  delta_t = 6 - 4.08 = 1.92 s
  starts at rest, falls under a = -9.8
  s2 = 0 + 0.5(-9.8)(1.92)^2 ~ -18 m

Step 4  Distance
  = |s1| + |s2|
  = 81.6 + 18
  = 99.6 m

Displacement = s1 + s2 = 81.6 - 18 = 63.6 m
The Split Method — Distance After Reversal
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the split method. Step one — find the reversal time by setting v equal to zero.
t-peak equals minus u over a, which is four point zero eight seconds. The ball reverses direction here.
Step two — displacement from zero to t-peak. Plug into the formula. Two hundred forty-three point two less eighty-one point six equals plus eighty-one point six metres. That is the ascent.
Step three — displacement from t-peak to six seconds. The ball starts at rest at the peak and falls for one point nine two seconds under minus nine point eight. Displacement is minus eighteen metres.
Step four — add absolute values. Eighty-one point six plus eighteen equals ninety-nine point six metres. That is the distance.

Notice the displacement from start to finish is simply the sum of the signed pieces — eighty-one point six minus eighteen equals sixty-three point six, which matches the direct formula. The split is only needed when you want the distance.

scene_composition.text:
Rule for every kinematic problem

The s in the formulas means
DISPLACEMENT, always.

To compute distance when direction
reverses, split into sub-intervals
where v has a single sign, and
sum the |displacements|.

When does v reverse?
  set u + a*t = 0
  -> t_reversal = -u / a

If u and a are same-sign, no reversal
within t > 0, so distance = |s|.

If u and a are opposite-sign, a
reversal DOES happen at t_reversal > 0.
Always check.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the rule to memorise. The s in all three kinematic equations means displacement. Always.
To compute distance when direction reverses, split the motion into sub-intervals where velocity has a single sign, and sum the absolute displacements.
To detect a reversal, check whether u and a have opposite signs. If they do, a reversal happens at t equals minus u over a. Compute that time and compare with the t in the problem.

If u and a have the same sign, there is no reversal, and distance equals absolute displacement. The plug-and-chug is safe. But always check first, because one wrong assumption invalidates the whole answer.

regeneration_variants.label: Braking car — no reversal
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Car at 20 m/s brakes at -4 m/s^2 for 4 s. t_reversal = 5 s (beyond 4 s window), so NO reversal within time. s = 20*4 - 0.5*4*16 = 80 - 32 = 48 m. Distance = displacement = 48 m.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same-sign u and a in the relevant window -> distance equals |displacement|

regeneration_variants.label: Object bouncing twice
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Beyond a single reversal, multi-bounce motion requires splitting into as many sub-intervals as reversals.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: One sub-interval per monotonic velocity segment
